confidence.disclosure: Abu Abd Allah Muhammad al-Idrisi al-Hammudi al-Hasani is famous for two things: his great geographical work the Nuzhat al-Mushtaq fi Ikhtiraq al-Afaq (commonly called Kitab Rujar, the Book of Roger) and his silver world map made for Roger II of Sicily. The basic facts of his life are clear (born in Ceuta, educated in Cordoba, worked at the Norman court in Palermo), but specific dates of his travels and personal events are largely missing. The journey is necessarily reconstructed from the geographical knowledge displayed in his work.

sources: S. Maqbul Ahmad, India and the Neighbouring Territories in the Kitab Nuzhat al-Mushtaq

journey.event: Born in 1100 in Ceuta on the North African coast opposite Gibraltar, into the displaced Hammudid family - Idrisid Sharifs descended from al-Hasan ibn Ali, who had ruled briefly in al-Andalus before being driven out by the Berbers.

Educated in Cordoba, the principal centre of learning in al-Andalus despite its political decline. Studies the religious sciences, Greek and Arabic geography, and the works of his predecessors al-Mas'udi, Ibn Hawqal, and the Balkhi school of geographers.

As a young man begins extensive travels around the western Mediterranean. Visits Lisbon and other towns of al-Andalus, North Africa, and (according to his own work) reaches the Atlantic coast of Morocco and possibly southern France.
Travels along the North African coast through the towns of the Maghrib. The descriptions of these regions in his later geographical work draw on first-hand observation as well as on earlier sources.

Continues his travels eastward, perhaps as far as Anatolia and the eastern Mediterranean. The accuracy of his geographical descriptions of these regions suggests personal travel, though some of it may also reflect detailed information from other travellers.

Around 1138 settles at the court of Roger II of Sicily in Palermo, an unusual cosmopolitan kingdom in which Norman rulers preside over a population of Greek Christians, Latin Christians, Arab Muslims, and Jews. Roger, fascinated by geography, commissions al-Idrisi to compile a comprehensive geographical work.

For about fifteen years works under Roger's patronage on a vast project. With the king's resources al-Idrisi summons travellers, merchants, and ambassadors from across the Mediterranean and beyond and systematically interviews them about the regions through which they have passed. The resulting database covers nearly the whole of the known world.

Supervises the construction of an enormous silver planisphere for Roger II - a circular world map about two metres in diameter, weighing perhaps 150 kg, on which the lands and seas of the inhabited world are engraved. The disc is the most ambitious cartographic project of the medieval period. (It will be lost in the riots that follow Roger's death.)

Completes the great companion volume to the silver planisphere on 14 January 1154 (about a month before Roger II's death): the Nuzhat al-Mushtaq fi Ikhtiraq al-Afaq (The Excursion of One Eager to Penetrate the Horizons), commonly known as Kitab Rujar, the Book of Roger. It contains 70 sectional maps and a detailed gazetteer covering everything from Iceland and the Baltic to Korea and East Africa.
journey.source: Al-Idrisi, Nuzhat al-Mushtaq; Encyclopaedia of Islam

journey.event: Roger II dies on 26 February 1154. His son William I (William the Bad) maintains al-Idrisi's patronage, but the political climate begins to deteriorate as anti-Muslim sentiment grows in the Norman kingdom.

In 1161 a revolt against William I in Palermo turns into anti-Muslim rioting; the silver planisphere is broken up by the rebels for its metal value, and many Muslim residents of Palermo are killed. Al-Idrisi (according to some accounts) flees back to North Africa around this time.

Dies in 1165 (560 AH), most probably in his birthplace Ceuta to which he had returned. The exact circumstances are unclear. His Nuzhat al-Mushtaq survives in several manuscripts, copied throughout the Islamic world; in the 16th century parts of it are translated into Latin and become the basis of European knowledge of much of Africa and the Indian Ocean. He remains the most influential geographer of the medieval Mediterranean.
